messages.content:
I have numerous workshets which contain formulas and vba codes.
To protect the formulas in the cells from bein deleted or changed, it is recommended that I protect the sheet.
When I do protect a sheet, sometimes the VBA code will fail because the sheet is protected.
Is there a way of still protecting my cell formulas yet still allowing my VBA codes to work correctly.

Yes, there is a way to protect your cell formulas while allowing your VBA code to work correctly. You can use the "UserInterfaceOnly" property of the "Protect" method in VBA to achieve this.

When you protect a sheet normally, it blocks all changes, including modifications made by VBA code. However, if you set the "UserInterfaceOnly" argument to "True" while protecting the sheet, it only restricts changes made through the user interface, while allowing VBA code to make modifications.

Here's an example of how to protect the sheet with the "UserInterfaceOnly" property set in VBA:

```vba
Sub ProtectSheet()
    ActiveSheet.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
End Sub
```

By using this method, your formulas will be protected, but your VBA code will still be able to modify cells and ranges on the sheet.

Note: Although this method allows your VBA code to modify cells, it doesn't prevent someone from accessing your VBA code via the VBA editor and making changes. If you want to protect your VBA code, you can password-protect your VBA project.
This suggestion sounds very promising and it might just be the solution I need. Can you expantiate further and explain how I should use it etc.

Certainly! Here's a step-by-step guide on how to use the "UserInterfaceOnly" property in VBA to protect your cell formulas while allowing your VBA code to work correctly:

1. Open your Excel workbook and go to the worksheet you want to protect the formulas on.
2. Press `ALT + F11` to open the VBA editor.
3. In the VBA editor, locate the worksheet module for the sheet you want to protect by expanding the "Microsoft Excel Objects" folder in the "Project Explorer" window and double-clicking on the relevant sheet.
4. In the worksheet module, insert the following code:

```vba
Private Sub Worksheet_Activate()
    Me.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
End Sub
```

This code will automatically protect the sheet whenever it is activated (i.e., clicked on or selected). 
If you want to run this code for multiple worksheets, you can duplicate the `Worksheet_Activate()` subroutine in each relevant worksheet module.

5. Close the VBA editor (`ALT + Q`) to return to the Excel worksheet.
6. Save your workbook as a Macro-Enabled Workbook (.xlsm) if you haven't already done so.

Now, whenever someone opens the workbook and selects the protected sheet, it will automatically be protected with the "UserInterfaceOnly" property set to "True". This means that they won't be able to manually modify the cells or formulas on the sheet, but your VBA code will still be able to make changes.

It's important to note that this method only protects the formulas from being deleted or modified through the user interface. To protect your VBA code, you should also password-protect your VBA project. You can do this by going to the VBA editor, selecting "Tools" from the top menu, choosing "VBAProject Properties," navigating to the "Protection" tab, and entering a password.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any further questions.
can I call this from a module

Yes, you can also call the code to protect the worksheet from a module. Here's how you can do it:

1. Open your Excel workbook and go to the module where you want to call the code.
2. In the module, insert the following code:

```vba
Sub ProtectWorksheet()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Sheet1") ' Replace "Sheet1" with the name of your worksheet
    
    ws.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
End Sub
```

3. Update the line `Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Sheet1")` with the name of the worksheet you want to protect. This line assigns the worksheet object to the variable `ws`.
4. You can call this subroutine from anywhere in your workbook to protect the specified worksheet. For example, you can assign this code to a button or a keyboard shortcut, or you can run it from the Immediate Window in the VBA editor.

When the subroutine `ProtectWorksheet` is executed, it will protect the specified worksheet with the "UserInterfaceOnly" property set to "True". This will allow your VBA code to modify cells and ranges on the protected sheet, while preventing manual changes through the Excel user interface.

Remember to save your workbook as a macro-enabled file type (.xlsm) so that the VBA code can be executed.

I hope this clarifies how to call the code from a module. Let me know if you have any further questions!

I presume that this will protect the entire worksheet and prevent user changes to any cell within the workbook. Is there a way to restrict this protection to only the cells that contain formulas, so that a cell without a formula can still be changed by a user

Yes, you can modify the code to protect only the cells that contain formulas while allowing users to change cells without formulas. Here's an example:

```vba
Sub ProtectFormulas()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim rng As Range
    
    Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Sheet1") ' Replace "Sheet1" with the name of your worksheet
    Set rng = ws.UsedRange.SpecialCells(xlCellTypeFormulas)
    
    ws.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
    rng.Locked = True
End Sub
```

In this updated code, the `UsedRange.SpecialCells(xlCellTypeFormulas)` method is used to identify all cells containing formulas on the worksheet. The `rng` variable is then set to this range.

After protecting the worksheet with `UserInterfaceOnly := True`, the `rng.Locked = True` line is included to lock only the cells that contain formulas, preventing user changes to those cells. Cells without formulas will remain unlocked and editable by users.

Remember to modify the line `Set ws = ThisWorkbook.Worksheets("Sheet1")` to specify the name of your worksheet.

By calling the `ProtectFormulas` subroutine, it will protect the worksheet and lock only the cells with formulas, allowing users to modify cells without formulas.

I hope this meets your requirements! Let me know if you have any additional questions.
Can you please write this code to work with the Active Sheet

Certainly! Here's an updated code that will work with the active sheet:

```vba
Sub ProtectFormulas()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim rng As Range
    
    Set ws = ActiveSheet
    Set rng = ws.UsedRange.SpecialCells(xlCellTypeFormulas)
    
    ws.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
    rng.Locked = True
End Sub
```

In this code, the `Set ws = ActiveSheet` line sets the "ws" variable to the currently active sheet. This way, you can run the code while working with any sheet, and it will protect only that active sheet.

By calling the `ProtectFormulas` subroutine, it will protect the active sheet and lock only the cells with formulas, allowing users to modify cells without formulas.

Feel free to let me know if there's anything else I can assist you with!
Using the code you have suggested, is there a way of notifying the user when they try to change a cell that contains a formula, that it is protected and changes can not be made

Yes, you can use the `Worksheet_Change` event in VBA to notify the user when they try to change a protected cell that contains a formula. Here's an example of how you can implement it:

1. Open the VBA editor by pressing `ALT + F11`.
2. In the Project Explorer window, double-click on the worksheet module where you want to implement this notification. For example, double-click on "Sheet1" if that's the sheet you want to work with.
3. In the worksheet module, paste the following code:

```vba
Private Sub Worksheet_Change(ByVal Target As Range)
    Dim rngProtected As Range
    Dim intersectRange As Range
    Dim cell As Range
    
    Set rngProtected = Me.UsedRange.SpecialCells(xlCellTypeFormulas)
    Set intersectRange = Intersect(Target, rngProtected)
    
    If Not intersectRange Is Nothing Then
        Application.EnableEvents = False
        For Each cell In intersectRange
            cell.ClearContents
        Next cell
        Application.EnableEvents = True
        
        MsgBox "You cannot change a protected cell with a formula.", vbExclamation, "Protected Cell"
    End If
End Sub
```

4. Save and close the VBA editor.

Now, whenever a user tries to change a protected cell that contains a formula, the code will clear the contents of that cell and display a message box with the notification that the cell is protected.

Note that if you have multiple protected cells with formulas on the sheet, this code will clear the contents of all the intersecting cells in one go.

Feel free to modify the notification message or customize the code further based on your specific requirements.

I hope this addresses your need for notifying users when they try to change protected cells with formulas. Let me know if you have any further questions!

When I apply the VBA code below to my worksheet, the cells without formulas can not be changed.
I get a message " that the cell is on a protected sheet an that I need to unprotect it to make any changes.
I only want the cells that contain formulas to be protected.

Dim ws As Worksheet
Dim rng As Range
Set ws = ActiveSheet
Set rng = ws.UsedRange.SpecialCells(xlCellTypeFormulas)
ws.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
rng.Locked = True

Apologies for the confusion. To ensure that only cells with formulas are protected, and cells without formulas remain editable, you need to set the "Locked" property of cells without formulas to "False." 

Here's an updated version of the code that will protect the cells with formulas and leave the other cells unlocked:

```vba
Sub ProtectFormulas()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim rng As Range
    Dim cell As Range
    
    Set ws = ActiveSheet
    Set rng = ws.UsedRange
    
    'Unprotect the sheet
    ws.Unprotect
    
    For Each cell In rng
        If cell.HasFormula Then
            cell.Locked = True
        Else
            cell.Locked = False
        End If
    Next cell
    
    'Protect the sheet with UserInterfaceOnly
    ws.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
End Sub
```

This updated code loops through each cell in the used range of the active sheet. It checks whether the cell contains a formula using the `HasFormula` property. If a cell contains a formula, it is locked (`cell.Locked = True`), and if it doesn't contain a formula, it is left unlocked (`cell.Locked = False`).

After running this code, cells with formulas will be protected and cannot be modified, while cells without formulas will remain editable.

I apologize for any confusion caused. Please let me know if you have any further questions or concerns!

Yes, you can certainly protect only specific cells with formulas while leaving the rest of the worksheet editable. Here's an example code that demonstrates how to achieve this:

```vba
Sub ProtectFormulas()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim rngFormulas As Range
    Dim rngUnprotected As Range
    
    Set ws = ActiveSheet
    Set rngFormulas = ws.UsedRange.SpecialCells(xlCellTypeFormulas)
    
    'Specify the range of cells you want to leave unprotected
    Set rngUnprotected = ws.Range("A1:C10")
    
    'Unprotect the sheet
    ws.Unprotect
    
    'Lock all formula cells
    rngFormulas.Locked = True
    
    'Unlock the specified range of cells
    rngUnprotected.Locked = False
    
    'Protect the sheet with UserInterfaceOnly
    ws.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
End Sub
```

In this code, you'll need to modify the `Set rngUnprotected = ws.Range("A1:C10")` line to specify the range of cells you want to leave unprotected. You can adjust this range to cover the specific cells or ranges that should be editable.

After running this code, the cells with formulas within your specified range (`rngFormulas`) will be protected and cannot be modified, while the cells in the `rngUnprotected` range will remain editable.

Make sure to adjust the range accordingly to meet your specific requirements.

If you have any further questions or need additional assistance, feel free to ask!
when the code runs, I get an error 'Unable to set the Locked property of the Range class'

The error "Unable to set the Locked property of the Range class" suggests that there might be certain cells within your specified ranges that are merged cells. Merged cells cannot have individual properties set, such as the "Locked" property.

To address this issue, you can modify the code to first unmerge any merged cells within the specified ranges before applying the "Locked" property. Here's an updated version of the code that includes this modification:

```vba
Sub ProtectFormulas()
    Dim ws As Worksheet
    Dim rngFormulas As Range
    Dim rngUnprotected As Range
    Dim cell As Range
    
    Set ws = ActiveSheet
    Set rngFormulas = ws.UsedRange.SpecialCells(xlCellTypeFormulas)
    
    'Specify the range of cells you want to leave unprotected
    Set rngUnprotected = ws.Range("A1:C10")
    
    'Unprotect the sheet
    ws.Unprotect
    
    'Unmerge any merged cells within the specified ranges
    rngFormulas.MergeCells = False
    rngUnprotected.MergeCells = False
    
    'Lock all formula cells
    For Each cell In rngFormulas.Cells
        cell.Locked = True
    Next cell
    
    'Unlock the specified range of cells
    rngUnprotected.Locked = False
    
    'Protect the sheet with UserInterfaceOnly
    ws.Protect UserInterfaceOnly:=True
End Sub
```

This updated code includes a loop that iterates through each cell in the `rngFormulas` range and sets the "Locked" property to `True` individually. Additionally, the code includes the `UnmergeCells` property to unmerge any merged cells within the specified ranges.

By unmerging cells before setting the "Locked" property, you should be able to protect cells with formulas and leave the specified ranges editable.

If you have any further questions or encounter any issues, feel free to let me know!
